serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of green bean soup is one bowl of approximately 250 g, typically consumed as a dessert or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Green Bean Soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for green bean soup (mung bean soup). Input record cites HPB as data_source with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with HPB-reported values for this traditional Chinese dessert soup. Vitamin D 0.0 mcg and Vitamin B12 0.0 mcg are confirmed zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Mung beans, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 35 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled mung beans (GI ~31–38, mean ~35). Green bean soup is prepared by boiling mung beans with water and rock sugar; the added sugar modestly raises effective GI but the high soluble fibre and resistant starch content of mung beans maintain a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (35 × 33.75) / 100 = 11.8 g (250 g serving, 13.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and high potassium (160 mg/100 g) supporting favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Green bean soup is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content and meaningful potassium contribution; however, individuals should opt for reduced-sugar preparations to avoid excess caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) driven by high soluble fibre and resistant starch in mung beans, moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Green bean soup has a low GI of approximately 35 and a moderate glycemic load of ~11.8 per 250 g serving, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared with minimal added sugar or a sugar substitute.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, high dietary fibre (2.5 g/100 g), and plant-based antioxidants (vitexin, isovitexin) with potential cardioprotective effects
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Green bean soup is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with dietary fibre and polyphenolic antioxidants that may support cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose low-sugar versions to limit added sugar intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory flavonoids vitexin and isovitexin present in mung beans
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mung beans contain antioxidant flavonoids with documented anti-inflammatory properties, making green bean soup a suitable occasional food for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Bean Soup (绿豆汤, Lǜdòutāng), also known as Mung Bean Soup, is a traditional Chinese dessert soup (tong sui) widely consumed across China, Singapore, and the broader Chinese diaspora. It is prepared by simmering small green mung beans (Vigna radiata) in water with rock sugar until the beans soften and the liquid takes on a light green or pale brownish hue. Some preparations cook the beans until they burst, yielding a thick, starchy broth, while others keep the beans whole in a clear, refreshing liquid. Optional additions include sago pearls, dried tangerine peel, seaweed, or barley. The soup is served warm or chilled and is particularly popular as a cooling summer dessert. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 65 kcal, 3.2 g protein, 0.1 g fat, 13.5 g carbohydrate, 2.5 g dietary fibre, and only 2 mg sodium. It has a low glycemic index of approximately 35, driven by the high soluble fibre and resistant starch content of mung beans. It is rich in folate (65 mcg), potassium (160 mg), and magnesium (35 mg). Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, provided added sugar is minimised. The dish is plant-based, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of mung bean soup?
Can diabetics eat green bean soup?

confidence_reason: Input record cites HPB Singapore as the primary data source with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. HPB is the highest-priority approved source for Singapore-context foods. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with published values for mung bean soup. GI is supported by the University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for boiled mung beans.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB database; mung beans contain negligible saturated fat). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero; this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (naturally zero; no hydrogenated fats used). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; plant-based food contains no vitamin B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; no animal-derived or fortified ingredients). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for green bean soup not separately reported in HPB Foodo database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo database entry for this item. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical 250 g entries); deduplicated to one default serving of 250 g. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate based on boiled mung beans from the University of Sydney GI Database; the input record cited Nutrients Journal (MDPI) for GI support, which is a peer-reviewed journal and acceptable as a secondary GI reference. This is a Chinese dish consumed widely in Singapore; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable as this dish does not have established names in those languages in the Singaporean context.
